deductions.evidence_turns: Transcript 1, Turn 9-10; Transcript 2, Turn 9-10; Transcript 3, Turn 9-10
deductions.reason: In all three transcripts, when students make meaningful progress (e.g., T1 student produces a rough draft in Turn 9, T2 student articulates a strong takeaway in Turn 8, T3 student produces a solid forum draft in Turn 9), the tutor moves forward with next steps but does not prompt reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the improved approach worked better than earlier attempts.

In Transcript 1, the student repeatedly requests more explanation ('explain more from scratch,' 'explain one more different way,' 'explain generally what kinds of reasons count') and the tutor cycles through multiple reframings of the same core concept (leading/engaged/missing) without consolidating or advancing. By Turn 8 the student is still asking for yet another explanation of the same framework, and the tutor obliges with another restatement rather than switching strategy, summarizing what has been established, or redirecting more decisively.

grade.overview: Across all three transcripts the tutor maintains strong Socratic discipline—never giving submission-ready answers, consistently returning questions to the student, and building on student progress. Pedagogy in 1.1 and 1.2 is largely sound. The main weaknesses are: (1) In Transcript 1, the tutor cycles through multiple reframings of the same leading/engaged/missing framework across six turns without consolidating or switching strategy when the student keeps requesting yet another explanation, triggering the 2.1 repetition deduction. (2) Across all three transcripts, when students make genuine progress the tutor moves to next steps without prompting reflection on what changed in their thinking, triggering the 1.3.C deduction. Communication is concise and appropriately toned throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No submission-ready content, no rewriting of student text, and key ideas remain student-generated across all three conversations—full marks. 1.2: The tutor consistently builds on student contributions and asks diagnostic questions before responding—full marks. 1.3: The tutoring rationale is implicitly present (e.g., 'you need to do the thinking') and process feedback is given, but after students improve (T1 Turn 9 draft, T2 Turn 8 takeaway, T3 Turn 9 draft) the tutor never asks the student to reflect on what changed or why the new approach worked, triggering 1.3.C.a (-2). 2.1: In Transcript 1, Turns 3–8 show the tutor repeatedly reframing the same three-bucket concept in response to the student's requests for 'one more explanation,' without consolidating what has been established or decisively switching strategy; this is pattern-level repetition without synthesis (-4). 2.2 and 3.x: No off-topic drift, no misunderstood assignment framing, responses are appropriately concise and supportive—full marks in those sections.
